faqSubtitle: Everything you need to know about converting PDF to JPG.
faq1Question: Is this PDF to JPG converter completely free?
faq1Answer: Yes, entirely free. There are no hidden fees, no premium tiers for JPG conversion, and no account required. Upload your PDF, download your JPGs — that's it.
faq2Question: What resolution will my JPG images be?
faq2Answer: You get to choose. We offer three quality options: Standard (150 DPI) for web and general use, High (300 DPI) for professional and print use, and Maximum (600 DPI) for archiving or when you need the finest detail possible.
faq3Question: How many pages can I convert at once?
faq3Answer: You can convert PDFs with up to 200 pages in a single upload. Each page becomes its own JPG file. If you have more than 200 pages, split the PDF first and convert each part separately.
faq4Question: Will the colors and text look the same in JPG?
faq4Answer: In the vast majority of cases, yes. Our rendering engine processes each page faithfully, preserving colors, fonts, and layouts accurately. The only exception is that JPG doesn't support transparency — any transparent areas will be filled with white.
faq5Question: Is my PDF safe to upload? Who can see it?
faq5Answer: Only you. Your PDF is sent to our secure servers over an encrypted connection, converted, and automatically deleted within 60 minutes. We don't sell, share, or retain your files beyond what's strictly needed for conversion.
faq6Question: Can I use the JPG files commercially?
faq6Answer: Absolutely. The JPG images you download belong entirely to you. Use them for personal projects, client work, or commercial purposes — we make no claims on your converted files.
seoSection1Title: When Should You Convert PDF to JPG?
seoSection1Para1: JPG is one of the most universally compatible image formats in the world. It opens in every browser, every image viewer, every phone, and every design tool — without needing any special software. When you need to share a page from a PDF, embed it in a presentation, upload it to a website, or use it in a document where PDFs aren't accepted, converting to JPG is the practical solution.
seoSection1Para2: Our converter gives you genuine quality control. At 300 DPI, the output is sharp enough for professional printing. At 150 DPI, file sizes stay small and manageable for email and web use. At 600 DPI, you get archival-grade detail that holds up under close inspection or large-format printing. Whatever your use case, there's a setting that fits.
seoSection2Title: JPG vs PNG — Which Should You Use?
seoSection2Para: JPG and PNG are both great image formats, but they serve different purposes. JPG is ideal when file size matters — it compresses images efficiently, making it perfect for sharing, uploading, and everyday use. PNG is better when you need lossless quality or transparency support. For most PDF-to-image use cases — presentations, social media, documents, web pages — JPG is the right choice. If you need transparent backgrounds or pixel-perfect accuracy for design work, use our PDF to PNG converter instead.
seoSection3Title: Common Uses for PDF to JPG Conversion
seoSection3Para: Professionals convert PDF reports and charts into JPGs for embedding in PowerPoint slides. Marketing teams extract product images from design PDFs for use on websites and social media. Teachers pull diagrams and illustrations from course materials for use in other formats. Lawyers and archivists save scanned documents as JPG images for records management systems. Developers extract visual assets from technical documentation. Whatever your reason, SmallPDF.us handles the conversion quickly, privately, and for free — no account needed.
